title: ‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?” He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them And he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me  “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea. Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire. And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.
scripture_ref: Matthew 18v1-9. Mark 9v33-37. The disciples argue part 4
devotional:
Mark 9v33-37.

‘’They came to Capernaum. When he was in the house, he asked them “What were you arguing about on the road?” But they kept quiet because on the way they had argued about who was the greatest. Sitting down, Jesus called the Twelve and said “Anyone who wants to be first must be the very last, and the servant of all.” He took a little child whom he placed among them. Taking the child in his arms, he said to them, ‘’Whoever welcomes one of these little children in my name welcomes me; and whoever welcomes me does not welcome me but the one who sent me.”

'The Little ones.'

Many bible scholars believe that Jesus is referring to children when he speaks of the 'little ones' in Matthew 18:6, so they apply this passage as to how we should treat young children. I think to apply this to young children is a secondary application, not it's primary application. Jesus was not thinking about children's ministry, or children per-say when he spoke these words, rather he was continuing to address the issues of the disciples pursuit for greatness. The ‘little ones’ in this context are anyone who suffers because of the pursuit of greatness by an other. In verses 5-11 Jesus describes the opposite heart attitude to what the disciples expressed when they asked the question. 'Who is the greatest?' With this in mind then: -

How do we avoid the self-seeking attitudes of the disciples?

We need to receive with the right attitude. What did Jesus have in mind when he was speaking about receiving people? In the New Testament there are two different words for receive. The most common Greek word is lambano, receive, here having the meaning 'take into one's possession, to acquire.' Archelaus went to Rome to receive his appointment as king over the opposition of his subjects who had also appealed to Rome. The word Jesus uses in this context is dechomai. It is used in the Greek as a term for those in love or an intimate embrace. Jesus was speaking of warmth here, an embracing and friendly compassion from the heart.

What kind of person would be unable to embrace another? What kind of person couldn't show this kind a warm authentic welcome? Someone whose heart was set on their own agenda, someone whose focus was on themselves.

How we treat Jesus is seen in how we treat people. 'And whoever welcomes a little child like this in my name welcomes me.' The kind of relationship we have with Jesus is seen in the kind of relationships we have with those around us. If we are loving and kind to people, then that is an indication that we are expressing love to Jesus. If we are cold and distant from people that is an indication that we are cold and distant towards Jesus. We cannot be one way towards people and another way towards God.

We must receive people in Jesus' name. In the bible a person's name was more than a title, it was an expression of their characters, so to receive someone in Jesus' name is to receive someone as an expression of the character of Jesus. So our hearts and arms are open to all as an expression of the reality of Jesus.

Jesus' warning!!

Before we look at this warning, there are a number of things we need to notice.

Jesus is alone with his disciples when he spoke these words. He was still in the house in Capernaum dealing with the sinful attitudes of his disciples. It would be very easy for some to use these verses as a means of not taking responsibility for their own sin. Although Jesus talks about others causing 'little ones to sin' he is not in any way taking responsibility from the 'little one's' for their own sinful behaviour. 'But if anyone causes one of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for him to have a large millstone hung around his neck and be drowned in the depth of the sea.'

What do we need to learn from this verse.

We are a) people of influence, irrespective of our personality type, our abilities and our opportunities. We all have some kind of influence on others. The word Jesus uses to describe the process of causing someone to sin is an interesting one in the Greek language skandalon. It actually started out to be a word that describes the part on a trap that you would use to put the bait. In fishing, it would be the fishing hook. In a mousetrap, it would be the little tab that sticks up where you put the cheese on. Some older versions translated the word stumbling block.

Jesus is saying that every time someone is wrongly influenced by us leading him or her to sin, it is as if we have set a trap for them. The sin that Jesus is referring to is very specific to this context, however this specific sin is the motive or the source of all other sin.

So what specific sin is Jesus alluding to in verse 6? I think Jesus is talking about any sin that robs a person of their childlike vulnerability and dependence. When we become independent, ceasing to appreciate and enjoy the father's love we automatically veer towards sinful habits in order to find life and love. When people begin to look for life and love through pursuing sinful habits, it is because they have been robbed of freedom. The sin that Jesus speak of here always robs people of freedom; they are caught -in a trap; a skandalon.

Every time we encourage some to move away from grace, and begin to teach legalism, we are becoming a stumbling block to others. It is the responsibility of every Christian leader to fight to maintain people's freedom in Christ.

Jesus continues in v.6b by saying 'It would be better for him to have a large millstone hung around his neck and to be drowned in the depth of the sea.' The type of 'millstone' to which Jesus referred was the large stone that required a donkey or ox to turn. It usually weighed several hundred pounds. Romans sometimes practised this form of execution. Jesus is implying that we would be better dead, than be the cause of someone loosing their freedom and their intimate relationship with God. Imagine the shock that came over the disciples when they heard this. In their dispute over whom was the 'greatest' they had just caused each other to sin or stumble by inciting envy, jealousy and anger. Jesus is like a good parent that says; 'Dear help you if you hurt one of my children.'

We need to take a step back and evaluate our lives and ministry in the light of this verse. We need to ask what kind of influence we are. We will be judged for our influence before we will be judged for our deeds. To be passive, our inactivity does not mean that you are not some kind of influence. Not to be lovingly involved with encouragement and affirmation in the life of others is to be stumbling stone. Do people know and love Jesus more because they have met you?
prayer: Father may my life be a continual godly influence on those people I encounter every day.
html:
‘’At that time the disciples came to Jesus and asked, “Who, then, is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven?” He called a little child to him, and placed the child among them And he said “Truly I tell you, unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. Therefore, whoever takes the lowly position of this child is the greatest in the kingdom of heaven And whoever welcomes one such child in my name welcomes me “If anyone causes one of these little ones—those who believe in me—to stumble, it would be better for them to have a large millstone hung around their neck and to be drowned in the depths of the sea. Woe to the world because of the things that cause people to stumble! Such things must come, but woe to the person through whom they come! If your hand or your foot causes you to stumble, cut it off and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life maimed or crippled than to have two hands or two feet and be thrown into eternal fire. And if your eye causes you to stumble, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to enter life with one eye than to have two eyes and be thrown into the fire of hell.

Study Text: Matthew 18v1-9. Mark 9v33-37. The disciples argue part 4

Bible in a year reading plan: - 2 Samuel 1:1-3:39
